perturbation.original_text: FUSION shall pay such invoice within [***] of receipt of the invoice in accordance with the Supply Agreement
perturbation.changed_text: FUSION shall pay such invoice within 90 days of receipt of the invoice in accordance with the Supply Agreement
perturbation.explanation: Extended payment terms beyond standard practices, creating potential financial risk and deviating from standard commercial invoice terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Truth in Lending Act (TILA) aims to promote the informed use of consumer credit by requiring disclosures about its terms and cost. While this contract may not explicitly involve 'consumer credit,' extended payment terms can de facto function as a form of credit. Setting a 90 day payment period without clear disclosure of potential interest or late fees could obscure the true cost of the transaction, conflicting with the spirit of TILA's disclosure requirements regarding credit terms.

perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the payment term to 90 days, potentially conflicting with the Truth in Lending Act (TILA). The Law Explanation suggests that extended payment terms without proper disclosure of interest or late fees can obscure the true cost of the transaction, thus contravening TILA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies TILA as pertaining to 'CONSUMER CREDIT PROTECTION,' and Scraped Law Snippet 2 reinforces this by listing 'Truth in Lending Act' and related sections under 15 U.S.C., suggesting it aims to ensure transparency in credit-related transactions. The Perturbation Explanation indicates that the change deviates from standard commercial invoice terms and presents financial risk, supporting the contradiction as it can function as a form of undisclosed credit.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice or other communication required or permitted to be given hereunder shall be in writing and shall be delivered in person, transmitted by facsimile, e-mail or similar means of recorded electronic communication or sent by registered mail, charges prepaid, addressed to the addresses set out on the signature page hereof. Any such notice or other communication shall be deemed to have been given and received on the day on which it was delivered or transmitted (or, if such day is not a business day, on the next following business day) unless mailed, in which case on the [***] following the date of mailing; provided, however, that if at the time of mailing or within [***] thereafter there is or occurs a labour dispute or other event that might reasonably be expected to disrupt the delivery of documents by mail, any notice or other communication hereunder shall be delivered or transmitted by means of recorded electronic communication as described.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice or other communication required or permitted to be given hereunder shall be in writing and shall be delivered in person, transmitted by facsimile, e-mail or similar means of recorded electronic communication or sent by registered mail, charges prepaid, addressed to the addresses set out on the signature page hereof. Any such notice or other communication shall be deemed to have been given and received only when actually received by the addressee.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the receipt of notice to 'only when actually received' removes the legal presumption of receipt upon mailing, potentially contradicting established legal principles regarding notice in contract law and increasing ambiguity and the risk of disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC 1-202(e) establishes the principle of 'presumption' regarding receipt of a notice.  It outlines that if certain conditions are met (e.g., proper mailing), the notice is presumed to have been received even if there is no confirmation of actual receipt.  The modified text, stating notice is effective 'only when actually received,' directly contradicts this presumption, shifting the burden of proof and potentially invalidating clauses relying on the presumption of notice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-202. Notice; Knowledge. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-202. Notice; Knowledge. (a) Subject to subsection (f), a person has " notice " of a fact if the person: (1) has actual knowledge of it; (2) has received a notice or notification of it; or (3) from all the facts and circumstances known to the person at the time in question, has reason to know that it exists. (b) " Knowledge " means actual knowledge. "Knows" has a corresponding meaning. (c) " Discover ", " learn ", or words of similar import refer to knowledge rather than to reason to know. (d) A person " notifies " or " gives " a notice or notification to another person by taking such steps as may be reasonably required to inform the other person in ordinary course, whether or not the other person actually comes to know of it. (e) Subject to subsection (f), a person " receives " a notice or notification when: (1) it comes to that person's attention; or (2) it is duly delivered in a form reasonable under the circumstances at the place of business through which the contract was made or at another location held out by that person as the place for receipt of such communications. (f) Notice, knowledge, or a notice or notification received by an organization is effective for a particular transaction from the time it is brought to the attention of the individual conducting that transaction and, in any event, from the time it would have been brought to the individual's attention if the organization had exercised due diligence. An organization exercises due diligence if it maintains reasonable routines for communicating significant information to the person conducting the transaction and there is reasonable compliance with the routines. Due diligence does not require an individual acting for the organization to communicate information unless the communication is part of the individual's regular duties or the individual has reason to k
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: and Uniform Warehouse Receipts Act in 1906; the Uniform Bills of Lading Act and Uniform Stock Transfer Act in 1909; and the Uniform Conditional Sales Act in 1918. The ULC officially took on the task of drafting a comprehensive code to provide guidelines for all commercial transactions in 1940. In 1942, the ULC and the American Law Institute joined in a partnership that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the notification receipt terms to 'only when actually received,' removing the presumption of receipt upon mailing. This directly contradicts UCC 1-202(e), which, as explained in the Law Explanation, establishes that notice is presumed to be received under certain conditions (e.g., proper mailing), even without confirmation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this, stating that a person 'receives' a notice when 'it is duly delivered in a form reasonable under the circumstances.' The original text aligns with this presumption, while the changed text eliminates it, creating a direct contradiction and potentially invalidating clauses that rely on the presumption of notice.

perturbation.original_text: Following the Term, this Supply Agreement shall automatically renew for successive periods of one (1) year (each a "Renewal Term"), unless a Party delivers written notice of non-renewal to the other Party [***] prior to the end of the applicable term.
perturbation.changed_text: Following the Term, this Supply Agreement shall automatically renew for successive periods of one (1) year (each a "Renewal Term"), unless a Party delivers written notice of non-renewal to the other Party 1 day prior to the end of the applicable term.
perturbation.explanation: Providing only 1 day for notice of non-renewal is unreasonable and could be deemed unconscionable, contradicting principles of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 205 states that every contract imposes a duty of good faith and fair dealing in its performance and enforcement. Requiring notice of non-renewal only 1 day prior to the end of the term is arguably a violation of this duty, as it does not provide the other party with a reasonable opportunity to make alternative arrangements, thereby undermining the principle of fair dealing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by requiring only 1 day's notice for non-renewal, contradicts the principle of good faith and fair dealing. The Perturbation Explanation states that this short notice is unreasonable. The Law Explanation further elaborates that such short notice does not provide the other party with a reasonable opportunity to make alternative arrangements. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that good faith encompasses 'observance of fair dealing standards.' By drastically reducing the notice period, the modified contract arguably fails to observe fair dealing standards, thus contradicting the implied duty of good faith.
